candidate_problems.text: Can the notion of 'which branch contains a given observer-state' be made non-circular and objectively well-defined? Decoherence yields approximate, basis-dependent, coarse-grained quasi-classical structure, but the conjecture relies on branches being robust enough to ground definite records and self-location. The open problem is whether there is a principled criterion for branch identity, persistence, and observer-copy individuation across time, rather than an FAPP notion that may be too vague to bear ontological and probabilistic weight.

Can the Born rule be derived from self-locating uncertainty without smuggling in probabilistic assumptions or decision-theoretic norms that already presuppose amplitude-squared weighting? The conjecture shifts 'why this outcome?' into indexical uncertainty, but then needs a non-question-begging account of why rational expectation over successor observers should track branch weights specifically, rather than branch counting or some other measure. This remains a central unresolved tension because the explanatory success of the whole picture depends on it.

What exactly is the metaphysical and epistemic status of the pre-measurement observer's anticipation of future copies, and when does self-locating uncertainty legitimately arise? If all successors exist, it is unclear how to formulate uncertainty before branching, after branching, or across continuous decoherence, and how personal identity, confirmation, and empirical testing work in such a framework. The open question is whether indexical uncertainty is a genuine explanatory replacement for stochastic outcome selection or only a redescription that leaves the phenomenology of chance underexplained.
